options.why: Placed only $1$ decimal place instead of the $2$ that come from $3.11$.
Placed $3$ decimal places, one more than the two factors give.
Left the whole-number digit product without placing any decimal point.

questions.solution_text:
$3.11$ has $2$ decimal places and $6$ has none, so the product has $2$.
$3.11\times6=18.66$

options.why: Counted only the $2$ places of $5.34$ and ignored the $1$ place of $1.2$.
Counted only the $1$ place of $1.2$ and ignored the $2$ places of $5.34$.
Placed $4$ decimal places, one more than the two factors give.

questions.solution_text:
$5.34$ has $2$ decimal places and $1.2$ has $1$, so the product has $3$.
$5.34\times1.2=6.408$

options.why: Placed only $1$ decimal place, so no leading zero was inserted to reach $2$.
Placed $3$ decimal places, inserting one leading zero too many.
Left the digit product without placing any decimal point.

questions.solution_text:
$0.4$ and $0.2$ each have $1$ decimal place, so the product has $2$.
$8$ needs a leading zero to fill $2$ places.
$0.4\times0.2=0.08$

options.why: Placed $4$ decimal places, one more than the two factors give.

questions.solution_text:
$2.25$ has $2$ decimal places and $1.2$ has $1$, so the product has $3$.
$2700$ with $3$ decimal places is $2.700$.
$2.25\times1.2=2.7$

options.why: Placed only $3$ decimal places, missing one of the needed leading zeros.
Placed only $2$ decimal places, counting just one factor's decimals.
Placed $5$ decimal places, inserting one leading zero too many.

questions.solution_text:
$0.25$ and $0.04$ each have $2$ decimal places, so the product has $4$.
$100$ needs leading zeros to fill $4$ places: $0.0100$.
$0.25\times0.04=0.01$
